coredata.dc:title: Composite phase change materials with carbon foam and fibre combination for efficient battery thermal management: Dual modulation roles of interfacial heat transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hanxue
Zhang, Guanhua
Yan, Xiaoyu
Dou, Binlin
Zhang, Daquan
Cui, Guomin
Yang, Qiguo
coredata.dc:description: An efficient battery thermal management system (BTMS) can undoubtedly improve the performance and lifetime of lithium-ion batteries. In this study, a novel battery thermal cooling module (BCM) consisting of composite phase change material (CPCM) with carbon foam skeleton support material and a carbon fiber thermally conductive gasket (CFT) are proposed. Experimental results show that the combination of CPCM loaded on carbon foam and CFT provides significant cooling and temperature balancing benefits to the battery components, with peak temperature reductions of 25.13% and 27.83% at 1.3C and 1.5C, and maximum temperature differences for 1.5C remaining within 2.7 °C. The high enthalpy of the CPCM is the key to battery cooling. The addition of CFT reduces the interfacial thermal resistance and enhances phonon heat transfer, effectively controlling the uniformity of the battery surface temperature. Therefore, this study shows that the combined BTMS with BCM and CFT has a good temperature response and cooling effect. The BTMS achieves the dual modulation of CPCM with high enthalpy and carbon material with enhanced heat transfer, enabling intelligent and long-term stable operation. This material combination is expected to have a wide range of applications in battery materials, energy storage and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite phase change material
Carbon fibre thermally conductive gasket
Battery thermal management system
Air thermal resistance
Phonon transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process and thermal test parameters for the BTMS.
Enthalpy curves of samples S1 to S9 (a). TG curve (b), FT-IR spectrum (c), and XRD pattern (d) of CPCM sample S2.
The density (a), loading rate (b), thermal conductivity (c) and thermal stability (d) of different BCMs.
Surface temperature curves of the battery without and with the BCM at discharge rates of 0.7C (a1, 2), 1.0C (b1, 2), 1.3C (c1, 2), and 1.5C (d1, 2).
Surface temperature curves of the battery in the presence of CFT and PU-F, CFT and C–F or CFT and Cu–F under discharge at 0.7C (a1, 2, 3), 1.0C (b1, 2, 3), 1.3C (c1, 2, 3), and 1.5C (d1, 2, 3).
Maximum temperature curves (a1-4), minimum temperature curves (b1-4), and temperature differences (c1-4) of the BTMS at different discharge rates (0.7C, 1.0C, 1.3C, 1.5C).
Diagram of the interfacial heat transfer process.
Components of composite phase change materials.

head.ce:title: Composite phase change materials with carbon foam and fibre combination for efficient battery thermal management: Dual modulation roles of interfacial heat transfer

University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Materials Protection and Advanced Materials in Electric Power, Shanghai University of Electric Power, Shanghai 200090, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient battery thermal management system (BTMS) can undoubtedly improve the performance and lifetime of lithium-ion batteries. In this study, a novel battery thermal cooling module (BCM) consisting of composite phase change material (CPCM) with carbon foam skeleton support material and a carbon fiber thermally conductive gasket (CFT) are proposed. Experimental results show that the combination of CPCM loaded on carbon foam and CFT provides significant cooling and temperature balancing benefits to the battery components, with peak temperature reductions of 25.13% and 27.83% at 1.3C and 1.5C, and maximum temperature differences for 1.5C remaining within 2.7 °C. The high enthalpy of the CPCM is the key to battery cooling. The addition of CFT reduces the interfacial thermal resistance and enhances phonon heat transfer, effectively controlling the uniformity of the battery surface temperature. Therefore, this study shows that the combined BTMS with BCM and CFT has a good temperature response and cooling effect. The BTMS achieves the dual modulation of CPCM with high enthalpy and carbon material with enhanced heat transfer, enabling intelligent and long-term stable operation. This material combination is expected to have a wide range of applications in battery materials, energy storage and other fields.

ce:def-description.ce:para: the CPCM loading rate on the skeleton material
the mass of the skeleton material
the total mass of the BCM
maximum temperature of battery discharge curves
minimum temperature of battery discharge curves
temperature difference of battery discharge curves
BCMs filled with S2 CPCM and copper foam
BCMs filled with S2 CPCM and carbon foam
BCMs filled with S2 CPCM and polyurethane
CPCM samples with the mass fraction of SAT from less to more (0%, 5%, 45.75%, 51.5%, 71.5%, 81.5%, 84%, 86.5%, 91.5%)

ce:section.ce:para: Nearly 30% of global greenhouse gas emissions come from conventionally-fueled road transport, which significantly contributes to air quality and environmental problems worldwide [1]. Therefore, clean energy vehicles, which can be operated without the direct release of harmful substances into the environment, have become a major research focus. Electrochemical batteries are used for energy storage in electric vehicles, with lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) being the most widely used today. LIBs are promising candidates due to their high energy density, low self-discharge rate, long cycle life, and high discharge voltage [2–4]. During vehicle use, the battery discharges and generates considerable heat due to the electrochemical reactions in the battery. Therefore, this generated heat needs to be discharged to maintain the battery at a safe operating temperature [5,6]. Many studies have shown that if the temperature of a LIB exceeds 50 °C, the life cycle and rechargeability of the battery are significantly reduced [7]. Safe operating temperatures are typically reported to be within the range of 20–40 °C [8]. Exposing batteries to higher temperatures can reduce their cycling performance and lead to thermal runaway and eventually battery explosion. Furthermore, the maximum temperature difference range when a battery is in operation should be limited to 5 °C [9]. Battery packs face several potential problems, such as overcharging, over-discharging, or short-circuiting due to uneven battery charging or discharging. In addition, temperature inhomogeneity increases with increasing discharge rate [10].

The thermal problems associated with safe battery operating conditions have been addressed by battery thermal management systems (BTMSs) [11,12]. In most cases, BTMS design varies depending on the workpiece used to dissipate the heat from the battery [13,14]. Many studies have investigated battery cooling with air and liquid as the cooling media [15–17]. However, some of the issues associated with air/liquid-cooled BTMSs are the need for air/liquid distribution systems and pumping accessories as well as high power consumption [18]. The application of phase change materials (PCMs) in BTMSs reduces the complexity of the system components. Wang et al. [19] developed a numerical model to calculate the performance of the battery thermal management system by analyzing the heat generation process of the battery and the heat transfer theory of the PCM. Their study found that the incorporation of PCM can effectively reduce the maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference of the battery. Jilte, Ravindra et al. [20] investigated the comparative cooling performance of a battery thermal management system with PCM over an ambient temperature range of 30 °C–40 °C under external natural convection conditions. By combining nano-PCM and external natural convection cooling, the battery temperature could be kept below 46 °C. Jilte, Ravindra D., et al. [21] presented simulation results for a Li-ion battery module operating at a higher ambient temperature of 40 °C. At an initial temperature of about 25 °C, the temperature rise was up to 40 °C. This was similar to the wide range of ambient temperatures (from 20 °C to 55 °C) proposed by Fathabadi [22], who compared combined PCM cooling in free convection and forced convection modes and showed that such a system can limit the temperature of a battery module to below 60 °C.

Some researchers have also used multiple layers of phase change materials to cool batteries [23,24]. Although this strategy has been effective for battery cooling, organic phase change materials have mainly been used [25,26]. Organic phase change materials suffer from leakage problems, and most applications that use these materials require an additional wrapping material on the outside of the phase change material. This leads to a significant reduction in thermal conductivity. Organic phase change materials also have the disadvantage of being flammable, which easily causes fires if thermal runaway occurs in a battery. The use of inorganic phase change materials has therefore become a new research direction [27]. The main inorganic phase change materials are hydrated salts, whose heat of phase change depends on the strength of bonding between separated water molecules [28,29]. The phase change temperature of hydrated salts can range from a few degrees Celsius to over 100 °C. The main hydrated salt phase change materials include magnesium chloride hexahydrate [30], sodium sulfate decahydrate [31,32], sodium carbonate heptahydrate [33], and sodium acetate trihydrate [34]. However, most phase change materials inevitably suffer from phase changes, supercooling, and lower thermal conductivity during phase change. Consequently, different additives and controlled mass fractions have been added to improve their properties [35,36]. Sodium acetate trihydrate (SAT) has received increasing attention among phase change energy storage materials [37–39] due to its suitable phase change temperature (58.4 °C) and high latent heat of phase change (264 kJ/kg). SAT is also characterized by a low coefficient of phase change expansion, good chemical stability, non-toxicity, and low cost. Unsurprisingly, as a hydrated salt phase change material, SAT suffers from high subcooling, phase delamination, low thermal conductivity (0.4–0.7 W/(m·K)) [40–42], and corrosion when paired with commonly used metals (copper, aluminum, and stainless steel) [43]. A great deal of research has been reported on the preparation of composite phase change materials. Different nucleating agents, thickeners, and phase change thermoregulators have been experimentally investigated for application to SAT matrices to overcome the drawbacks of SAT to some extent [44–46].

The most common method used for BTMS cooling is the use of a heat sink in thermal contact with the heat source, which allows the conduction of heat around the BTMS. The heat sink is typically a highly thermally conductive material such as copper or aluminum. However, this method is challenged by the lack of good contact on the thermal contact surface. If thermal contact is poor, heat does not sufficiently flow from the heat source to the heat sink, resulting in low heat sink performance. Placing a thermal interface material (TIM) between the heat source and the heat sink is an effective strategy for improving heat dissipation [47]. High thermal conductivity and low interfacial thermal resistance are the most critical properties when selecting a TIM [48–50] to ensure optimal and stable operating conditions as well as a long BTMS lifetime [51–54]. TIMs mainly consist of polymers and various thermally conductive fillers [55–57], such as metals [58], carbon fibers [59], and nanomaterials [60]. There is relatively less research into the use of carbon fiber thermally conductive spacers for electronic device cooling applications. Therefore, carbon fiber was chosen in this work to reduce the interfacial thermal resistance of a BTMS and to improve the BTMS performance.

Based on this research background, this paper reports the preparation of a combined composite phase change material (CPCM) and carbon material cooling module for use in a BTMS. The thermal performance of the CPCM was investigated by experimentally applying disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (DHPD) to the SAT compositions. CPCMs were added to the copper foam, carbon foam, and polyurethane foam skeleton materials, and the entire composite was used as a battery cooling module (BCM) for temperature control experiments during battery discharge to determine the suitability of the cooling module in a BTMS after reducing the interfacial thermal resistance by a carbon fiber thermally conductive gasket (CFT). By adding a CFT, the efficiency of phonon heat transfer is improved to achieve a rapid heat transfer response. The CPCM achieves the dual modulation of high enthalpy and carbon material-enhanced heat transfer for the intelligent and stable long-term operation of the BTMS. This study provides practical guidance for the design and optimization of thermal management structures for Li-ion batteries.

SAT, DHPD, nano alumina, and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) were supplied by Shanghai Maclean. The copper foam used in this experiment was supplied by Suzhou Jiashida Metal Foam Co. Nano alumina was a highly effective nucleating agent and excellent thermal conductive filler. Copper foams with 85% and 95% porosity and a pore density of 50 ppi were used in the comparison experiments. The other two skeleton support materials were carbon foam with 85% and 95% porosity and 23D polyurethane, which were purchased from Shengshi Jingxin New Materials Co, Ltd. and Nanjing Yongsheng Sponge Factory, respectively. The CFT, which was the additional thermal interface material, was purchased from Waermtimo. This CFT had a thickness of 0.2 mm and a thermal conductivity of 10 W/(m·K). The square battery used in this experiment was purchased from Tianjin Lishen Battery J/S Co.,Ltd. and its exact dimensions are indicated in Fig. 1 . The battery used in the experiment is a Lithium ion polymer rechargeable cell with a nominal voltage of 3.85 V (0.2C mA discharge), a nominal capacity of 4590mAh (0.2C mA discharge) and a max continuous discharge power of 17.76 W.

All substances were accurately weighed and mixed. For experimental accuracy, the mass of each sample was set at 20 g. The proportions of the components in the samples are shown in Table 1 (in Supporting Information), and the required mass of each substance was weighed on an electronic balance. During the preparation of the CPCMs, a total of 9 samples S1–S9 were prepared according to the mass fraction of SAT from less to more (0%, 5%, 45.75%, 51.5%, 71.5%, 81.5%, 84%, 86.5%, 91.5%), with the mass fraction of DHPD decreasing accordingly. The mass fractions of CMC and nano alumina were kept unchanged at 3.5% and 5% (wt%). SAT was first added to a flask held in an oil bath maintained at 110 °C. This flask was stirred and heated for 10 min. Next, DHPD was added to the flask, and the mixture was stirred and heated for an additional 10 min. Finally, alumina nanoparticles and CMC were added. Stirring was performed at 1100 r/min to ensure that the samples were well-mixed, and the flask was sealed during stirring. Finally, the obtained CPCM was cooled and solidified at room temperature.

In order to cool the batteries, the skeleton material was cut into 50 mm × 80 mm rectangles, and the thicknesses of the three support materials were unified at 5 mm. A melt impregnation method was used to prepare a battery cooling module (BCM) containing a composite phase change material. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1.

The phase change behavior of the CPCM was characterized using a differential scanning calorimeter (Netzsch DSC-200PCPhox, Germany) at a heating rate of 2 °C/min. All tests were carried out at room temperature and in a nitrogen-protected environment. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Bruker D8-Advance over the 2θ range of 5–80°. The chemical structure of the CPCM was investigated by a Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet IS 10). The thermal stability was characterized by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA4000, PerkinElmer) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in N2.

BCMs were prepared using copper foam, carbon foam and polyurethane as the matrix material and filled with CPCMs. 95% and 85% porosity types were chosen for copper and carbon foam and 23D type was chosen for polyurethane. In total, 5 different types of BCM were prepared. They were tested for thermal conductivity, PCM loading rate and thermal stability. These BCMs were filled with S2 component CPCM to form the copper foam (Cu–F), carbon foam (C–F) or polyurethane foam (PU–F) PCM cooling modules required for the battery cooling experiments.

Thermal conductivity tests were carried out on the prepared PU-F, C–F, and Cu–F. An electromechanical thermal conductivity meter (DZDR-S, Nanjing Dazhan) was used to determine the thermal conductivity of the cooling modules. Influenced by sample size and conductivity, the instrument accuracy was ±3%. Measurements were taken at 5 min intervals, and each measurement was taken 5 times to reduce the influence of the external environment and the instrument itself on the experimental results.

The CPCM loading rate on the different skeletal materials was obtained with an electronic balance by separately weighing the mass of the skeletal material before adding CPCM and that of the prepared BCM. This is shown in Equation (1):
(1) α=(M2-M1)/M1
where α is the CPCM loading rate on the skeleton material, M1 is the mass of the skeleton material, and M2 is the total mass of the BCM.

Preparing a BCM with good thermal stability is a prerequisite for the effective use of a BTMS. The 85% porosity Cu–F, C–F, and PU-F were selected and stored in a vacuum oven at 45 °C. Photographs were taken every 30 min to observe the changes in the BCM.

The temperature of the battery surface was measured by Omegas T-type thermocouple, as shown in Fig. 1. Five measurement points (a, b, c, d, e) were set up on the battery, and these points were located at each of the two pole lugs and each quarter of the battery. The temperature of the discharged surface of the battery was measured at different discharge rates (0.7C, 1.0C, 1.3C, 1.5C, C is used to indicate the charge/discharge capacity rate of the battery. 1.0C indicate the current strength of the battery when fully discharged in 1 h) before and after the placement of the BCM and after the addition of the CFT. To further improve the efficiency of heat transfer, the TIM was placed between the surfaces of the BCM and the battery contact in the experiment. All battery discharge tests were carried out in a constant-temperature chamber to ensure insulation and constant temperature, and the ambient temperature was always maintained at 25 °C. The variation of heat and heat flux generated by LIB in the process of charge and discharge conforms to the continuity equation, momentum equation and energy equation [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the characteristics of CPCM
ce:section.ce:para: Nine sets of samples (S1 to S9) were obtained by adjusting the ratio of the SAT and DHPD components in the composite phase change material. The enthalpies of these nine samples were examined and the phase change parameters were obtained, as listed in Table 2 (in Supporting Information). Fig. 2 (a) clearly shows two distinct peaks at low SAT levels, indicating that two substances were present, and these substances underwent phase change processes at different temperatures. When the mass fraction of SAT reached 71.5%, only one melting peak was observed on the curve. This demonstrated that the phase change behavior of the CPCM was completely influenced by the phase change process of DHPD and SAT. Of the nine samples, sample S2 had the largest enthalpy of 278.9 kJ/kg, and the phase change onset temperature of S2 was 46.9 °C. After S2, the enthalpy of the CPCM tended to increase, but S7 showed a sudden drop. The combined mass fraction of SAT and DHPD had a significant negative impact on the enthalpy of CPCM at this point, resulting in a sudden drop in enthalpy. The phase change temperature point of sample S2 was near the battery alert temperature of 45 °C, and the enthalpy of this CPCM was also the highest of the prepared samples. Therefore, the CPCM used for the copper foam-based BCM was prepared with proportions corresponding to sample S2.

TG analysis of sample S2 is shown in Fig. 2(b). This analysis showed that the main mass loss temperature range for the CPCM was between 100 °C and 300 °C, which was mainly due to the decomposition of DHPD and the evaporation of crystalline water. However, a small CPCM mass reduction occurred below 100 °C, mainly due to the dissipation of crystallized water from the compound. This is the main reason why inorganic PCMs need to be encapsulated to enhance battery cycling performance. FT-IR was used to study the chemical structure of sample S2, as shown in Fig. 2(c). For CPCM, the peaks at 3341 cm−1 corresponded to the O–H stretching vibration. The peaks at 1641, 1553, 1409, and 1064 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C=O, –CH3, –CH, and C–O, respectively. The peak at 985 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of P=O. The XRD pattern of sample S2 is shown in Fig. 2(d). The positions of the main CPCM diffraction peaks were the same as those of pure SAT, DHPD, and Al2O3, which confirmed that the CPCM preparation process had no significant effect on the crystal structures of the component materials. No additional peaks were observed, implying that only physical interactions existed between SAT, DHPD, and Al2O3. This was beneficial for maintaining the thermophysical properties of the CPCM.

BCMs were formed through the capillary action of the skeleton and the high surface tension of the polyurethane skeleton, which could therefore be loaded with more CPCM [62]. Theoretically, a lower density means a smaller specific surface area and a weaker adsorption capacity. The densities of the five skeleton materials are shown in Fig. 3 (a). As shown in Fig. 3(b), the CPCM had the highest loading rate on PU-F and there was an order of magnitude difference in loading compared with the other two skeleton materials. This indicated that the polyurethane structure was the most conducive to being filled with CPCM. The CPCM loading rate on PU-F was 25.51, which was almost 8 times that of 85% C–F and 12 times that of Cu–F. A comparison of the loading rates of Cu–F and C–F at the two porosities only showed an insignificant difference. Although a reduction in porosity led to a reduction in loading rate, the reduction was very small. Therefore, it was assumed that porosity did not significantly affect the CPCM loading rate. Compared to the skeletal structure of the reticulated copper and carbon foam, the reticulated structure of the polyurethane had good flexibility and toughness and a greater contact surface with the CPCM. However, the capillary effect was enhanced by the presence of fine carbon particles on the surface of PU-F, and the flexible structure of PU-F was able to wrap more CPCM by changing its shape after the CPCM was formed into a solid. In contrast, the rigid structures of C–F and Cu–F did not wrap the CPCM in the solid state, and the loading rate was influenced by the solidified volume of the CPCM. Thus, the CPCM loading rates on C–F and Cu–F were much lower than that on the PU-F.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the thermal conductivity of Cu–F and C–F increased as their porosity decreased. PU had the lowest thermal conductivity of 0.537 W/(m·K). The 85% porosity C–F and Cu–F had thermal conductivities of 1.96 W/(m·K) and 2.98 W/(m·K), respectively. These were higher than the corresponding thermal conductivities of 95% porosity C–F and Cu–F, which were 0.617 W/(m·K) and 1.89 W/(m·K), respectively. This was because the skeleton material was thermally conductive, while the CPCM had a very low thermal conductivity relative to the skeleton material. Thus, the BCM mainly relied on the skeleton material for thermal conduction. This meant that a lower porosity and denser skeleton material led to more rapid thermal conduction. Copper foam and carbon foam, which are excellent conductors of heat, exhibited much higher thermal conductivities than polyurethane.

All three prepared BCM samples maintained good thermal stability during continuous heating at 45 °C. Fig. 3(d) shows that the surface morphology of the BCMs did not change over time. Compared to the samples at 0 min, no obvious cracks or melting leaks were observed on the surfaces of the samples at 210 min. Therefore, the BCMs showed good suitability for long-term use in a BTMS. Taking the thermal conductivity analysis as well as the load factor into account, copper foam with 85% porosity and foam carbon were selected to prepare the BCM for subsequent battery cooling experiments.

If a battery temperature exceeds 45 °C during battery discharge, it might stop working and explode, interrupting stable battery operation. Fig. 4 shows the surface temperature variation of a battery discharged at different rates without the addition of the BCM. With the increasing discharge rate, the surface temperature of the battery also increased. As shown in Fig. 4(a1), the maximum temperature on the surface of the battery reached 45 °C at 0.7C in the absence of the BCM. As shown in Fig. 4(b1), when discharged at 1.0C, the surface temperature of the battery rose to 57 °C and exceeded 45 °C before the battery was discharged for more than the usual length of time. Fig. 4(c1) shows that at the end of the discharge process, the battery temperature reached 65 °C, and Fig. 4(d1) shows that the maximum temperature reached 70 °C. A comparison of the graphs in Fig. 4(a1) to (d1) shows that the difference in temperature between the battery surfaces was not large, with a maximum temperature difference of 4 °C. This temperature difference gradually increased as the discharge rate increased. Moreover, the greatest increase in battery surface temperature was observed when the discharge rate was increased from 0.7C to 1.0C, with the battery surface temperature increasing by 12 °C (from 45 °C to 57 °C). In contrast, when the discharge rate was increased from 1.0C to 1.3C, the battery temperature increased by 8 °C, and when the discharge rate was increased from 1.3C to 1.5C, the battery temperature increased by only 5 °C.

Battery discharge leads to a significant amount of generated heat, causing the surface temperature of the battery to rapidly rise in a short period of time [63]. A cooling module is therefore placed on battery surfaces to alleviate the rapidly rising surface temperature. In this work, the BCM prepared with a copper foam backbone material had the highest thermal conductivity. Therefore, Cu–F with 85% copper foam loaded with CPCM was chosen for the BCM used in the BTMS. Using this BTMS, the maximum surface temperature of the battery at the end of the discharge process at 0.7C did not exceed 45 °C, as shown in Fig. 4(a2). Furthermore, the maximum temperature for the 1.0C discharge process was 48 °C, as shown in Fig. 4(b2). This indicated that the battery was basically discharged within the safe temperature. Thus, the maximum surface temperature of the battery was reduced by 9 °C. The maximum temperature at the end of the 1.3C discharge was 58 °C, as shown in Fig. 4(c2). This was a reduction of 7 °C compared to the battery without the cooling module. Fig. 4(d2) shows that the maximum temperature after discharge at 1.5C was 65 °C, which was a reduction of 5 °C compared to the battery without the BCM. As shown in Fig. 4(a2), the BCM was not effective in reducing the highest temperature of the battery discharged at 0.7C, but the temperatures of the batteries discharged at the other rates were reduced to around 35 °C. Thus, the battery was successfully maintained at an optimum temperature of 35 °C throughout the discharge process.

Comparing the minimum temperature at the surface of the battery with and without the BCM shows that the BCM reduced the surface temperature of the battery to a large extent at each discharge rate, rapidly absorbing the heat generated during battery discharge. Therefore, with this BCM, the battery was able to safely operate at discharge rates below 1.0C, and at rates greater than 1.0C, the BCM significantly reduced the surface temperature of the battery.

In the presence of the BCM alone, the battery temperature was successfully lowered, but the maximum temperature still exceeded the maximum temperature for safe operation. Moreover, it is worth noting that the BCM with Cu–F caused the temperature distribution on the surface of the battery to be very uneven. Achieving a uniform battery temperature distribution is also important for an effective BTMS. Therefore, to improve the temperature uniformity of the BTMS and to reduce the surface temperature difference, a CFT was added between the battery and the BCM.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the presence of the CFT resulted in a dramatic reduction in the temperature difference between the battery surface as well as a very uniform indexing scale. With the addition of the CFT and BCM, the batteries discharged at 0.7C and 1.0C had maximum surface temperatures within the safe temperature for battery operation, as shown in Fig. 5(a and b). At high discharge rates, batteries are more likely to generate a significant amount of heat in a short period of time, and the cooling effect of the BTMS is particularly important in this case. Fig. 5(c and d) shows that compared to the battery without BCM, a significant reduction in battery temperature occurred when the BCM is used. When discharged at 1.3C, the battery surface temperature did not exceed 50 °C, with the highest temperature point showing a reduction of more than 15 °C. When discharged at 1.5C, the temperature of the battery exceeded 45 °C for a significantly shorter period and the maximum temperature did not exceed 55 °C. Therefore, the surface temperature uniformity of the battery was much better after the addition of the CFT. However, regardless of the skeleton material chosen for the BCM paired with the CFT, the magnitude of the temperature difference on the battery surface increased with increasing discharge rate.

The high thermal conductivity of the CFT itself enhanced the efficiency of heat transfer through the BCM and allowed the combination of C–F and CFT to achieve excellent performance in the BTMS. The low thermal conductivity of PU-F meant that this combination was the least effective in terms of cooling, although the loading rate was not too high. Cu–F had the highest thermal conductivity of the three BCMs, but the temperature uniformity was not very good, which reduced the BTMS performance. C–F had roughly the same loading rate as Cu–F and a slightly lower thermal conductivity. However, the temperature difference on the battery surface using C–F and the CFT was significantly reduced compared to the battery cooled using Cu–F. The combination of C–F and CFT was therefore more suitable for achieving good BTMS performance.

The maximum (Tmax) and minimum (Tmin) temperature curves of battery discharge were selected for five cases: the original battery discharge without cooling management, battery discharge with a BCM, and battery discharge with both the BCM and CFT, as shown in Fig. 6 (a and b). The temperature difference (ΔT) was compared at different discharge rates, as shown in Fig. 6(c). Compared to the surface temperatures of the battery without cooling and the battery cooled with only the BCM, the addition of the CFT decreased the length of time required to reach the maximum temperature. Moreover, the time required to reach the maximum temperature for both Tmax and Tmin curves in the presence of the CFT was the longest for the original battery and the shortest for the BCM with Cu–F. This indicates that the combination of CFT and BCM rapidly absorbed the heat generated by the battery and transferred this heat into the CPCM, ensuring the safe and normal operation of the battery. The addition of the CFT significantly reduced the time required for the temperature to recover to 35 °C, indicating that all excess heat was absorbed at a faster rate. Thus, the BTMS temperature response was very effective at this point. At 1.5C discharge, the maximum temperature of the battery surface in the battery with the CFT and PU-F was 53.7 °C, which was the highest of the three Tmax curves. The maximum temperature of the minimum temperature curve for the battery with Cu–F was 46.8 °C. In the five cases, depending on the relationship between the maximum and minimum temperature and time, the battery cooling effect was ordered from lowest to highest as follows: battery without cooling < BCM only < PU-F and CFT < C–F and CFT < Cu–F and CFT.

The areas identified by the different colors in Fig. 6(c) represent the magnitude of the temperature difference in different BTMS tests. The temperature difference in each case decreased with increasing time, and the smallest temperature difference was achieved when PU-F was combined with the CFT. In this case, the same maximum and minimum temperatures were achieved at certain moments, showing that the temperature of the battery surface was uniform (ΔT = 0). The largest temperature differences occurred when the temperature reached its maximum point. After this, the temperature difference gradually decreased with time. The temperature difference increased with increasing discharge rate, with a maximum temperature difference of 9.1 °C at 1.5C for the battery only cooled with the BCM and a temperature difference of 2–3 °C with the addition of the CFT. From small to large, the temperature difference on the surface of the battery during discharge followed the order: PU-F and CFT < C–F and CFT < Cu–F and CFT < BCM only < battery without cooling. The addition of CFT helped significantly reduce the temperature difference in the BTMS, enhancing the performance of the BTMS and protecting the battery.

These results show that the combination of C–F and CFT provided significant cooling and temperature-balancing benefits to the battery components, with peak temperature reductions of 25.13% and 27.83% at discharge rates of 1.3C and 1.5C. Moreover, the maximum temperature difference remained within 3.6 °C at a discharge rate of 1.5C. Based on the cooling effect and the achieved temperature difference, it can be concluded that the combination of C–F and CFT resulted in the best cooling effect. This combination had the greatest and most effective impact on the BTMS and was able to guarantee the full performance of the battery to the greatest extent.

A diagram of the interfacial heat transfer process with the addition of the BCM and CFT is shown in Fig. 7 . Without the BCM, heat is mainly transferred through the air on the surface of the battery. When the BCM, which has a copper skeleton, is in contact with the battery, the excess heat is transferred to the CPCM through the copper foam skeleton [64–66]. With the addition of the CFT, the heat diffuses through the surface of the CFT and is then transferred to the BCM.

During BCM preparation, the CPCM starts as a liquid and then cools and solidifies into the skeleton material, creating an irregular solid surface. Irregular surfaces do not perfectly align with flat surfaces, meaning that air gaps exist. Therefore, when only the BCM is used, an air gap exists between the BCM and battery surfaces. The law of interfacial contact thermal resistance shows that when there is an air gap between two heat transfer interfaces, the actual heat transfer area decreases, the heat transfer pathways decrease, and the heat transfer rate sharply decreases. Meanwhile, the interface contact thermal resistance also greatly increases because the thermal conductivity of air is very low, which is not conducive to heat transfer. Therefore, even though copper has high thermal conductivity, the battery only cooled with a BCM does not demonstrate effective cooling. However, the addition of the CFT fills the air gap and increases the area available for heat transfer, which increases the number of heat flow paths available for heat transfer and the efficiency of heat transfer. The high thermal conductivity of the CFT itself also results in a significant increase in heat transfer. Therefore, the combination of CFT and BCM achieves a significant cooling effect.

Among the three skeleton materials, polyurethane foam is a flexible structure, while carbon foam and copper foam are rigid. The CPCM solidifies to a solid state and does not have a fixed rule of shape, so the flexible PU is better wrapped, and the structure of the skeleton undergoes a slight deformation with the CPCM, causing the CPCM to be more closely in contact with the skeleton. The CPCM only fills the skeleton voids and does not perfectly fit with the skeleton material, so the actual heat transfer contact area with the skeleton is lower. This explains the smaller temperature difference when PU-F was used compared with C–F and Cu–F.

It has been demonstrated that heat transfer between different solid surfaces is mainly achieved through the vibration of phonons [67–69]. Moreover, phonon transfer is stronger on continuous surfaces than on discrete particle surfaces. After the addition of the CFT, phonons are transferred from the cell surface to the CFT surface and then further transferred to the skeleton and CPCM surfaces. The continuous surface of the CFT enables a more rapid transfer of heat than direct contact with the CPCM surface. The high enthalpy of the CPCM is a major factor in enhancing the absorption of the heat released from the cell to achieve cooling. It can be seen that in the dual modulation BTMS combined with C–F and CFT, excellent heat absorption performance comes from the support of high enthalpy value of CPCM, and the enhanced heat exchange relies on the presence of CFT. The addition of CFT fills the gaps between the heat transfer surfaces, greatly reducing the air thermal resistance, while providing a continuous interface for phonon transfer, improving the heat transfer efficiency and creating a fast response in the BTMS.

In this study, a new BTMS cooling module with reduced interfacial thermal resistance and enhanced heat transfer via phonon vibration was designed to prevent the thermal runaway of Li-ion batteries. A CPCM with a large thermal storage capacity was well encapsulated with a skeleton support material using a melt impregnation method, and this was combined with a CFT for effective battery temperature control. The resulting CPCM-based combined cooling strategy effectively suppressed the temperature of the battery at various discharge rates. The temperature-induced phase change of the CPCM contributed to the temperature regulation capability of the combined C–F and CFT, which absorbed a significant amount of heat from internal battery. More interestingly, the CFT and C–F based combination also resulted in a more uniform battery temperature compared with the other CPCM-based BTMS combinations. The impressive cooling performance and fast thermal response of the BTMS designed using a CFT and C–F were attributed to the high thermal conductivity of the combined cooling materials and the excellent phase change heat absorption capability of C–F. This combination achieves the dual modulation of the high enthalpy in the CPCM and carbon material-enhanced heat transfer for the intelligent and stable long-term operation of the BTMS. However, the material forms currently used are still limiting and the future addition of flexible and liquid materials will allow the thermal management system to be applied in more variable environments. Therefore, CPCM-based BTMS designs capable of thermal regulation show great promise in the field of high-energy storage device thermal management and for alleviating the thermal runaway problems of Li-ion batteries under various conditions (fast charging, high temperatures, etc.).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51976126), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No.22ZR1442700, 22WZ2503100), and Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Committee of Shanghai outstanding academic leaders plan (No.21XD1402400).
